source.note: Regenerated batch with proper content-type mix: 1 carousel + 1 infographic + 3 text per author.

transcripts.topic: Operations-background CRO, AI-native services in heavy industries
Enterprise AI selling, MEDDPICC, decision criteria as a sales weapon

topics.title: The GTM Engineer Can't Replace Your RevOps Leader
topics.angle: The market is selling executives a fantasy that AI plus GTM engineers replaces the RevOps team. The layer that breaks is the translation of business strategy to tactical execution.
topics.sourceQuote: I remember in that same conversation having that conversation with a GoToMarket engineer, at one point he asked me what ARR was.

topics.title: Why, What, How: The Three Layers of GTM Work
topics.angle: GTM engineering is built for the How. CEOs and CROs own the Why. The VP of RevOps owns the What — that translation layer is where most AI-first orgs are about to discover they're missing critical infrastructure.
topics.sourceQuote: The go-to-market engineering layer is extremely capable of handling the how... I think that VP of RevOps role though translates all that into the what?

topics.title: AI Won't Cut Your Headcount Until You Fix Your Data
topics.angle: Boards mandating RevOps headcount cuts because AI are skipping a step. You can't layer agents on bad data architecture and inconsistent processes. The investment to reduce headcount has to happen BEFORE the headcount reduction.
topics.sourceQuote: In order for there to be a reduction in headcount, there has to be rigorous operations, a lot of standardization, and incredible data architecture in order to get there. It can't just replace people.

topics.title: Run the Business vs. Transform the Business: The Impossible Mandate
topics.angle: RevOps leaders are being asked to AI-transform the GTM org with the same headcount that's already drowning in forecasting, QBRs, comp, and territory planning. The math doesn't work.
topics.sourceQuote: How do I run the business while transforming the business? Well, all the priorities are gonna stay the same.

topics.title: The Eisenhower Matrix for AI-Enabled Operators
topics.angle: AI gives operators 10x output capacity, but the field can only absorb so much change. Doing 3 things that move the needle still beats doing 10 mediocre things.
topics.sourceQuote: Just because you can move so much faster, delivering three things really meaningfully... is still more important than delivering ten things that may or may not be adopted.

topics.title: Start Analog, Then Layer AI
topics.angle: The highest-quality AI workflow starts with you writing your hypothesis manually. AI then finds examples, crunches data, pressure-tests. Skip the analog step and the work collapses under the first probing question.
topics.sourceQuote: Start in analog mode, write your thoughts out manually... You can tell when somebody puts together a presentation that they didn't really do. They just AI slopped it together.

topics.title: Open a Google Sheet: The 4-Hour Exercise That Separates AI Operators from AI Tourists
topics.angle: If you're a senior analyst waiting for your company to enable you on AI, you've already lost. Open a Google Sheet, list recurring tasks, mark level of effort, ask what an agent could do. 4 hours puts you ahead of 90% of peers.
topics.sourceQuote: I would open up a Google Sheet and I would write down what are the core things that I do for my job that are repeating.

topics.title: An Operator Is Only 100% Honest in the Exit Interview
topics.angle: Three reasons companies hire consultants: bandwidth, expertise, and political cover. Sometimes the org needs someone outside the promotion chain to say the truth.
topics.sourceQuote: Really as an operator, the only time you get to be truly a hundred percent honest is in your exit interview.

topics.title: Do the Next Job Before You Get It
topics.angle: Promotion never comes if you're already not operating at the next level. AI is making this gap exponentially wider — top performers compound, everyone else stalls.
topics.sourceQuote: I'm gonna do the next job that I want before I get it.

topics.title: Operators Make Better Sellers Than Sellers Do
topics.angle: In operationally-intensive industries, classically-trained SaaS sellers get the demo through the door but lose the deal. I can teach an operator to sell. I can't teach a seller five years of industry experience.
topics.sourceQuote: Operators who then become sellers, which is how I think about hiring in these industries, actually lean towards people who have done the thing or operated in the space.

topics.title: The First Contract Is the Audition
topics.angle: In heavy industries, expansion isn't a product problem — it's a trust problem. The first deal is the cover charge for being asked bigger questions.
topics.sourceQuote: All the work actually happens after the first deal is closed. It's all about that's the audition is the first part.

topics.title: RevOps Is the Language of Rapid Scalability
topics.angle: RevOps isn't 'is Salesforce clean.' It's the language your company uses to test, measure, learn, and decide. Without it, you're not making decisions — you're guessing.
topics.sourceQuote: RevOps is the language in which companies test, measure, learn, and ultimately drive rapid scalability.

topics.title: Why Tight TAM Industries Punish Bad Delivery
topics.angle: When your industry is a duopoly of 20 logos, one botched implementation doesn't just lose a customer — it closes the entire market. Word of mouth in tight communities can replace your outbound machine. Or annihilate it.
topics.sourceQuote: The word of mouth impact from a referral... can be far more impactful than any outbounding strategy AI enabled or otherwise.

topics.title: AI-Native Services: The Quality Story Nobody Is Telling
topics.angle: Every AI narrative is about cutting headcount. The real story is scaling quality. The biggest bottleneck in services isn't hours — it's maintaining the founding team's standard as you grow.
topics.sourceQuote: The biggest bottleneck is scaling the quality. Typically when it starts, you have somebody really passionate, really skilled... And then it starts to erode as you grow and scale the team.

topics.title: We Got 10x Faster and Almost Lost Revenue
topics.angle: When we automated half our delivery time, our first reaction was panic — were we cannibalizing billable hours? Then customers found more work. The fear curve every services firm is about to ride.
topics.sourceQuote: We're spending half the time and we're not billing anymore for it. So we're actually losing revenue the better we do.

topics.title: The Build Order: CRO, Then RevOps, Then Enablement, Then AEs
topics.angle: Most founders' first GTM hire after themselves is an AE. That's why most early AEs fail. The non-negotiable build order: CRO, then RevOps + Enablement, THEN a single AE walks in.
topics.sourceQuote: When you hire me, there's an expectation you're also hiring somebody in rev ops and somebody in enablement. That's my team.

topics.title: Decision Criteria Is the Sales Weapon Nobody Talks About
topics.angle: The rep who waits for a buyer's decision criteria is a sitting duck. Buyers don't know how to buy AI. Build the editable scorecard. Hand it to them. The seller who shapes decision criteria can't lose.
topics.sourceQuote: The reps that influence decision criteria are the best reps, period.

topics.title: The CHRO Is No Longer the Economic Buyer for HR AI — IT Is
topics.angle: The classic enterprise sale used to terminate at the department head. AI changed that. IT now owns the architecture and can kill deals weeks after HR said yes.
topics.sourceQuote: HR has gone to get the deal done. IT has blocked the deal at the end of the process because they weren't included in the process itself.

topics.title: POCs Are the New Default — and Most Don't Convert
topics.angle: Risk, compliance, and governance kill POC-to-enterprise conversions at the finish line. If you're not absorbing security questionnaires upfront with a trust portal, you have a qualification gap that wrecks your forecast.
topics.sourceQuote: These POCs that are being implemented now tend to actually not convert to full enterprise deals because of risk compliance and governance.

topics.title: Forward Deployed Engineers Are Not a Trend — They're a Requirement
topics.angle: Every serious AI company has them. Selling agentic without an FDE on implementation is a bet against your customer's success. Bake the FDE-to-account ratio into your growth model.
topics.sourceQuote: Forward deployed engineers are in every one of these organizations. We have something similar.

topics.title: Stop Talking About Top of Funnel AI — Mid Funnel Is Where the Gold Is
topics.angle: LinkedIn is saturated with 10,000-step lead workflows. Meanwhile auto-generated SOWs from call transcripts, AI deal coaching tuned to your playbook, and GTM diagnostics that turn 2-week scoping into a 1-hour deliverable are eating the revenue ceiling.
topics.sourceQuote: What is really under discussed is how to leverage AI for mid funnel.

topics.title: Auto-Populate MEDDIC from Gong — Then Compare It to Rep Input
topics.angle: Reps inflate MEDDIC fields. The unlock isn't replacing rep input — it's auto-populating from Gong transcripts and running a compare-and-contrast. Triangulation surfaces bad reps, bad deals, and process gaps at once.
topics.sourceQuote: I came to our revops guy and I said, hey, is there a way for us to be able to auto-populate medic from Gong transcripts? And sure enough we actually got it syncing.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: AI-Native Services: The Quality Story Nobody Is Telling
posts.postText:
**Every AI services pitch is about cutting headcount. That's the boring half of the story — and the wrong half.**

The real unlock isn't doing the same work with fewer people.

It's scaling quality without it eroding as you grow.

Think about how a services firm actually decays. Day one, you have a founder or a senior operator who's brilliant, obsessive, and personally touches every deliverable. The standard is sky high.

Then you scale. You hire. You delegate. And the standard quietly slips — not because anyone is bad, but because you can't clone the person who set the bar.

That's the bottleneck in services. It was never hours. It was holding the founding standard across 50 people instead of 5.

This is where AI changes the math — and almost nobody is talking about it this way.

When you encode the senior operator's judgment into the workflow itself — the diagnostic logic, the QA pass, the way the best person on the team would pressure-test the work — every junior delivers closer to the founder's standard on day one.

The headcount story is real. But it's a cost story.

The quality story is the one that compounds.
posts.voiceNotes: Leans into Anthony's bold 'isn't X — it's Y' thesis hook and his services-as-software POV that quality, not hours, is the bottleneck. Operator-confident, no emoji on a thesis post per profile.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Start Analog, Then Layer AI
posts.postText:
**The highest-quality AI work I've seen always starts the same way: with a human writing the hypothesis by hand first.**

The people who skip that step produce work that collapses under the first hard question in the room. You can always tell. It's AI slop — confident, polished, and hollow.

Here's the order we actually use at LeanScale:

1. Start in analog mode. Open a doc, write your real thinking out manually — your hypothesis, your point of view, what you believe is true and why. No AI yet.

2. Hand AI the thesis, not a blank page. Now ask it to find supporting and disconfirming examples, pull the data, and stress-test your logic.

3. Make it argue against you. The most valuable prompt isn't 'help me build this' — it's 'here's my position, now tear it apart.'

4. Synthesize back into your own voice. You own the conclusion. AI accelerated the work around it; it didn't replace the thinking.

The difference shows up the moment someone probes the work.

Analog-first holds up under pressure because a human actually reasoned through it. AI-first folds — because nobody did.

The tool is incredible. But it amplifies your thinking. It doesn't substitute for it.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold thesis hook, numbered SOP-style steps in Anthony's tactical-playbook lane, lifts his actual 'AI slopped it together' phrasing and 'Here's the order we use at LeanScale' construction. Closes on his 'amplifies, doesn't substitute' register rather than a question.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: AI Won't Cut Your Headcount Until You Fix Your Data
posts.postText:
**Boards are mandating RevOps headcount cuts because AI is 'supposed to absorb the work.' They're skipping the most expensive step.**

You cannot layer agents on bad data and inconsistent process and expect savings. The agents just fail faster and louder.

The uncomfortable truth: the investment to reduce headcount has to happen *before* the reduction. Standardize the process. Rebuild the data model. Get the definitions agreed-upon and verifiable. Then the agent layer compounds.

Most leadership teams want the cost-out story without the capex story. It doesn't work that way.

I built the math into one image. Save it for your next board conversation about 'just using AI.'
posts.voiceNotes: Anthony's bold-thesis hook plus his 'skipping a step' reframe; sharp operator authority, no fluff. Single dense graphic that he points to ('save it for your next board conversation') in his INFOGRAPHIC register.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A two-column before / after comparison. Left column 'Cut first, backfill with agents' resolves to 'quietly rehiring under new titles.' Right column 'Fund data + process first' resolves to 'running 30-40% leaner.' Big headline across the top: 'Sequence is the whole game.' The visual point: the investment comes BEFORE the cut.

params.title: Sequence is the whole game.
params.body: Cut first, backfill with agents → quietly rehiring under new titles. Fund data + process first → running 30-40% leaner. The investment comes BEFORE the cut.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Why, What, How: The Three Layers of GTM Work
posts.postText:
**'We have an AI engineer now, so we don't need a RevOps leader.'**

I hear a version of this every week. It's how I know a GTM org is about to find out what's actually missing.

There are three layers to GTM work, and AI only collapses one of them.

The CEO and CRO own the Why. The VP of RevOps owns the What. GTM engineering owns the How.

Cut the What layer and you don't get a leaner org — you get a beautifully engineered system solving the wrong problem.

Swipe through for the breakdown of all three layers, what each one owns, and the exact place AI-first orgs are about to discover a gap.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with a quoted founder line (his pattern-recognition setup), lands the bold 'beautifully engineered system solving the wrong problem' tension. Caption baits the swipe; slides carry the Why/What/How framework Anthony articulated in the source quote. No emoji per thesis-post norm.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel breaking down the three layers of GTM work — the Why (CEO + CRO), the What (RevOps), the How (GTM engineering) — and the exact layer AI-first orgs cut by mistake.

params.title: Why, What, How.
params.body: The three layers of GTM work — and the one AI is fooling executives into cutting.

params.title: The Why — owned by CEO + CRO.
params.body: What market we attack, what the motion is, what winning means this year. Strategy. AI doesn't touch this.

params.title: The What — owned by VP of RevOps.
params.body: Translating strategy into process, data architecture, and measurement. The layer that scales you from 5 reps to 100.

params.title: The How — owned by GTM engineering.
params.body: Systems, integrations, agents, automations that make the What run. This is the layer AI actually collapses.

params.title: Cut the What and the How solves the wrong problem.
params.body: AI is raising the floor. It is not deciding what gets built, or why.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Operators Make Better Sellers Than Sellers Do
posts.postText:
**In operationally complex industries, the classically-trained SaaS seller gets the demo booked — and then loses the deal.**

We learned this the hard way, then turned it into a hiring rule.

The pattern was consistent. A polished rep would run a clean discovery, book the demo, and then hit a wall the second the buyer asked something that required actually having lived in their world. The buyer could feel the gap. Trust never formed. The deal stalled.

So we flipped how we hire for these motions.

Now we look for operators who became sellers — people who did the thing or ran in the space — instead of sellers we hope can learn the space.

Why? Because I can teach an operator to sell. The frameworks, the discovery, the deal mechanics — that's a few months of reps and coaching.

What I cannot teach is five years of having actually done the job the buyer is doing right now. That earns trust in the first ten minutes, and trust is the whole game in a complex sale.

The result: shorter cycles, higher win rates on the deals that matter, and a buyer who feels understood instead of pitched.

If you sell into a space where credibility is the bottleneck, stop hiring for the perfect pitch.

Hire for the scar tissue.
posts.voiceNotes: Bold contrarian thesis hook, then case-study arc (pattern → intervention → result → generalization) in Anthony's domain. Uses his 'Why? Because...' pivot and the verbatim 'I can teach an operator to sell / I can't teach five years of experience' logic. Lands on a sharp aphorism instead of a question.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Stop Talking About Top of Funnel AI — Mid Funnel Is Where the Gold Is
posts.postText:
Everyone is shipping top-of-funnel AI. Lead enrichment, sequencer agents, 47-step Clay tables.

Meanwhile the mid-funnel — where revenue actually closes — is wide open.

Here's what we build with daily at LeanScale to compress the middle:

𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗼-𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗦𝗢𝗪𝘀 — pulled straight from Fireflies transcripts, turned around in minutes instead of days.

𝗔𝗜 𝗱𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗰𝗼𝗮𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 — tuned to the customer's MEDDPICC and playbook, not a generic Gong scorecard.

𝗚𝗧𝗠 𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗴𝗻𝗼𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰𝘀 — 2-week scoping exercises collapsed into a 1-hour deliverable.

𝗤𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘀𝘆𝗻𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝘀 — discovery notes auto-mapped to the next-step plays that move a deal.

Great reps will still close. But the ones leaning on mid-funnel AI are forecasting cleaner and ramping quicker.

We'll break down the full mid-funnel stack in a future post.
posts.voiceNotes: Leans into Joe's bread-and-butter tool-stack listicle: Unicode-bold tool/workflow names with em-dash one-liners, 'what we build with daily' framing, named tools (Fireflies, Clay, Gong), salute sign-off, 1-2 hashtag bundle. No contrarian dunk.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Open a Google Sheet: The 4-Hour Exercise That Separates AI Operators from AI Tourists
posts.postText:
If you're a senior operator waiting for your company to "roll out AI," you've already fallen behind.

The people pulling ahead aren't waiting for a platform decision. They're spending 4 hours with a Google Sheet.

Here's the exercise we run with every new operator on the team:

𝟭. 𝗟𝗶𝘀𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 — Column A: every recurring task you do, daily through quarterly.

𝟮. 𝗦𝗰𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗲𝗳𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁 — Column B: low / medium / high level of effort.

𝟯. 𝗙𝗹𝗮𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲 — Column C: manual or automated today.

𝟰. 𝗔𝘀𝗸 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 — Column D: could an agent do this? yes / no / partially.

𝟱. 𝗠𝗮𝗽 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀 — Column E: if yes, what would the agent need access to?

That's it. No prompts, no tooling debate.

What you end up with is a personal automation roadmap that ranks itself — high-effort, high-frequency, fully-automatable tasks float to the top. Those are your week-one wins.

Four hours of this puts you ahead of most of your peers, because they're still consuming AI content instead of mapping their own work.

Full template's on docs.leanscale.team if you want to run it today. 🫡
posts.voiceNotes: Joe's primary content type. Numbered steps with Unicode-bold headers (matches his sample MEDDIC playbook), 'we run with every operator' team framing, 'That's it.' fragment for emphasis, docs.leanscale.team soft CTA, salute close. Avoids vulnerability and contrarian bait.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Forward Deployed Engineers Are Not a Trend — They're a Requirement
posts.postText:
Pattern we're seeing across the AI-native B2B startups we work with:

Every serious one has a Forward Deployed Engineer function. Or something that looks an awful lot like one.

The ones that don't are watching POCs die in production.

Why it matters for GTM:

Agentic products carry orders of magnitude more customer complexity at implementation than a normal SaaS rollout. Sales gets the deal across the line. The FDE keeps it from unraveling in week 3.

If you're modeling growth for an agentic product, the gearing ratio of FDEs to accounts belongs in the model right alongside AE quota capacity. Treat it as a CAC line, not a post-sale afterthought.

The AI-native services firms (us included) are building this muscle for our customers right now — pairing technical implementation horsepower with GTM strategists on every engagement.

Forward deployed isn't a hire. It's a stage of the customer journey.
posts.voiceNotes: Secondary fit for Joe. 'Pattern we're seeing' operator-to-operator opener, plural 'we' framing, sharp POV without a 'Stop doing X' dunk, concrete claim (gearing ratio, CAC line), salute close. Maps to AI-in-GTM and Engineering/Product role.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: The Eisenhower Matrix for AI-Enabled Operators
posts.postText:
My team's output ceiling jumped about 10x this year. Spending all of it at once was the mistake.

The field can only absorb so much change. Three things that ship and stick beat ten that ship and get ignored.

So we run every roadmap through one filter before we commit: level of effort against business impact — with an AI multiplier on the effort axis, because what used to be "high effort" is now cheap.

Built the whole thing into one image — save it for your next planning session.

#revops
posts.voiceNotes: Joe in matter-of-fact operator register. 'We run every roadmap through one filter' team framing, concrete 10x/3-vs-10 contrast, no contrarian dunk or vulnerability arc, salute close. Single dense 2x2 graphic fits the single-image INFOGRAPHIC constraint.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 2×2 prioritization matrix. X-axis: Level of Effort (with AI), Low → High. Y-axis: Business Impact, Low → High. Four labeled quadrants: top-left 'Quick Wins — ship now', top-right 'Big Bets — pick 1-2', bottom-left 'Auto / Delegate', bottom-right 'Drop'. Small footnote: 'AI shrinks the effort axis — re-score quarterly.'

params.title: Effort × Impact — with an AI multiplier.
params.body: Quick Wins: ship now. Big Bets: pick 1-2. Auto or delegate the low-impact lane. Drop the rest. AI shrinks the effort axis — re-score every quarter.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: The Build Order: CRO, Then RevOps, Then Enablement, Then AEs
posts.postText:
Most founders we talk to want to hire their first AE before they've hired their CRO.

It's the #1 reason early AEs fail.

We put together a simple visual on the non-negotiable build order for a B2B GTM org — and exactly where the wheels come off when you skip a step.

Swipe through to see:

The sequence we've watched work across 50+ hyper-growth startups (Mistral AI, Clio, Chainguard, and others)

The two roles that have to be in seat BEFORE the first AE

What ramp looks like when an AE hits a built machine vs. chaos

Where fractional RevOps fits early (and where it doesn't)

Prefer reading to swiping? The full breakdown's on docs.leanscale.team.

#revops #gtm

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Do the Next Job Before You Get It
posts.postText:
Best piece of career advice I ever got came from a friend who's climbed faster than anyone I know:

"Do the next job before you get it."

I'll be honest, the first time I heard it I filed it under motivational poster. Then I started paying attention to who actually got promoted around me, and who didn't.

The pattern was the same every single time.

The people who moved up were already operating a level above their title. Sitting in the meetings they technically didn't need to be in. Pulling in cross-functional partners to fix things nobody asked them to touch. Drafting the territory recommendation before anyone asked for one.

The people who didn't move up were doing exactly what their job description said. And doing it well. That was the whole problem.

What AI is doing right now is making that gap so much wider, so much faster. The operators opening a Google Sheet, testing agents, building workflows nobody requested are compounding by the week. The ones waiting to get enabled are watching the distance grow in real time.

If you're early in your career and you want to move fast, the advice still holds.

Figure out what your manager actually does all day. Then quietly start doing pieces of it.

The promotion is the lagging indicator. The work comes first.

If you're on a team like that right now, you already know what I mean. Cheers to the people who do the next job before anyone hands it to them.
posts.voiceNotes: Leans into Cameron's PERSONAL STORY primary lane — credits a friend instead of self-aggrandizing, sincere and teacherly, single-line-break paragraphs, warm 'Cheers' landing instead of a hard CTA.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: RevOps Is the Language of Rapid Scalability
posts.postText:
RevOps isn't "is Salesforce clean?"

It's the language your whole company uses to test, measure, learn, and decide.

Here's what I mean. Every call a GTM team makes — where to put reps, how to price, what to enable on, which segments to expand into — only works if everyone shares a way to measure it and talk about it.

That shared language is RevOps.

Without it, sales-first leaders jump straight to comp tweaks, enablement, and pricing experiments. All worthwhile moves. But you genuinely can't tell whether any of them worked, because there's no agreed-upon way to read the scoreboard.

From Sales and Marketing to Customer Success and Finance, RevOps is multiple departments playing in harmony. The RevOps leader is the conductor, making sure everyone is reading the same sheet music.

That's also why I'd push back on treating it as a reporting function. Reporting is the output. The language is the asset.

So if you're hiring your first RevOps person, don't ask "can they run reports?"

Ask "can they build the language the whole company uses to make decisions?"

That's the real unlock. Happy to talk it through if you're at that stage — let's connect!
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron's SOFT EDUCATIONAL secondary lane — fragment-as-correction opener, lifts his canonical 'playing in harmony' and 'reading the same sheet music' metaphors, calm teacherly register, soft 'let's connect!' close.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Run the Business vs. Transform the Business: The Impossible Mandate
posts.postText:
A lot of RevOps leaders I talk to are being handed a quietly impossible mandate right now:

Transform the GTM org with AI. Same headcount. And by the way, keep running forecasting, QBRs, comp, and territory planning exactly like before.

The trouble is, all the run-the-business work stays the same size. Nobody actually clears your calendar so you can go transform anything.

Here's the approach I'd defend for getting out from under it:

1. List the run-the-business work first. Forecasting cadence, pipeline reviews, comp administration, QBR prep, territory upkeep. Get it all on one page so you can see the real load.

2. Mark what's pure overhead. Be honest about which of those tasks are repeatable, rules-based, and low-judgment. That's your transformation surface area.

3. Pick exactly three things to genuinely move. Not ten. The field can only absorb so much change at once, and three things that land beat ten that nobody adopts.

4. Automate inside the run work, not around it. The win isn't a shiny new agent off to the side. It's compressing the forecasting prep and the QBR build so you buy back the hours.

5. Protect the calendar you free up. The minute you create slack, something will try to fill it. Defend that time for the transformation work or it evaporates.

You can't transform the business if you're drowning in running it. Make room first, then build.

Happy to walk through what that sequencing looks like for your team — let's connect!
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron's primary TACTICAL PLAYBOOK lane, downshifted to teacherly mode — numbered plain-symbol steps, no contrarian framing (the mandate is 'impossible' not 'everyone is wrong'), warm 'let's connect!' close. Echoes his 'do three things meaningfully' POV without copying the Eisenhower sample.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The Eisenhower Matrix for AI-Enabled Operators
posts.postText:
AI just handed every operator something close to 10x output capacity.

The catch nobody mentions: your field can only absorb so much change at once. Ship ten things and most of them never get adopted. Ship three that actually move the needle and they stick.

So I put the filter I'm using with our RevOps team into one image. Effort on one axis, business impact on the other, with a simple read on where AI genuinely accelerates the work versus where it still needs a human in the loop.

Save it for the next time your to-do list is longer than your week.

The goal was never to do more. It's to do the right three things, faster.
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron's calm-teacherly tactical register applied to a single shareable visual — 'do three things meaningfully' POV, 'with our RevOps team' team-first credibility, gentle 'save it' nudge rather than a hard CTA, no emoji.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 2×2 matrix titled 'The AI Operator's Priority Filter.' X-axis: Level of Effort, Low → High. Y-axis: Business Impact, Low → High. Quadrants: top-left 'Quick Wins' (low effort, high impact), top-right 'Big Bets' (high effort, high impact), bottom-left 'Fill-In Tasks' (low effort, low impact), bottom-right 'Time Sinks' (high effort, low impact). Mark the quadrants AI accelerates well with a check; mark the ones still needing human judgment with a caution symbol.

params.title: Effort × Impact, with an AI read.
items: Quick Wins — low effort, high impact (let AI run it)
Big Bets — high effort, high impact (human-led, AI-assisted)
Fill-In Tasks — low effort, low impact (batch or ignore)
Time Sinks — high effort, low impact (just stop)

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Why, What, How: The Three Layers of GTM Work
posts.postText:
There's a hiring trade founders keep trying to make: swap the RevOps leader for an AI engineer and call the org chart cleaner.

I get the appeal. One sharp technical hire, a stack of tools, fewer seats.

The piece that's missing is a layer most people don't have a name for. So I mapped the three layers of GTM work — the Why, the What, and the How — and exactly where an AI-first org is about to find a gap.

Swipe through. If you're rebuilding your GTM ops org right now, this is the conversation to have before the headcount one.
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron's collegial, non-combative framing of a sharp point — 'I get why it's tempting' softens what could be a contrarian take into a teaching moment, short caption pointing to the deck, forward-looking close instead of a hard sell. No emoji.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel mapping the three layers of GTM work — Why, What, How — and where an AI-first org is about to find a gap.

params.title: Why, What, How: The Three Layers of GTM Work.
params.body: And the one most AI-first orgs are about to discover they're missing.

params.title: The Why is owned by the CEO and CRO.
params.body: What market are we attacking, what does the motion look like, what does winning mean this year.

params.title: The What is owned by RevOps.
params.body: Territory model, capacity plan, comp design, lifecycle, forecast cadence. The layer that turns strategy into execution.

params.title: The How is owned by GTM engineering.
params.body: Which systems, integrations, agents, and automations make the What actually run at scale.

params.title: AI automates the How. It can't decide the What.
params.body: Hire both, be clear on what each owns. That's the conversation before the headcount one.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Run the Business vs. Transform the Business: The Impossible Mandate
posts.postText:
RevOps leaders are being asked to AI-transform the GTM org with the exact same headcount that's already drowning in forecasting, QBRs, comp, and territory planning.

The math doesn't work.

Nobody is removing a single thing from the run-the-business pile. Forecasting still happens. Comp still has to ship. The board still wants its QBR deck. And on top of all of that — "go re-architect the org around agents."

This is the KTBA vs PTNF problem at the worst possible scale.

Keeping the boat afloat is a full-time job. Pushing the needle forward is a second full-time job. AI doesn't merge them — it just makes the gap between the two more visible.

After 3-5 GTM leader conversations a week, the pattern is consistent:

→ The teams making real transformation progress carved out dedicated capacity for it — a person, a pod, a protected day.
→ The teams that bolted transformation onto an already-maxed team shipped neither. The run-work always wins, because it has a deadline.

Transformation isn't a stretch goal you assign on top of the day job.

It's a big rock. Treat it like one, or it never moves.
posts.voiceNotes: Leans into his KTBA-vs-PTNF and 'big rocks' operator argot, the '3-5 GTM leader conversations a week' cadence anchor, arrow bullets, and a clean declarative landing. Contrarian via reframe ('the math doesn't work'), not a label-opener.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Open a Google Sheet: The 4-Hour Exercise That Separates AI Operators from AI Tourists
posts.postText:
If you're waiting for your company to "enable you on AI," you've already fallen behind the people who didn't wait.

The ones pulling ahead aren't running a tooling evaluation. They spent four hours with a Google Sheet.

Here's the exercise I give every operator who asks me where to start:

1. Open a blank sheet. Column A: every recurring task you do — daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly. Be honest, include the boring ones.

2. Column B: level of effort. Low / medium / high.

3. Column C: KTBA or PTNF? Is this keeping the boat afloat, or pushing the needle forward?

4. Column D: could an agent do this against an agreed-upon definition? Yes / no / partially.

5. Column E: if yes — what inputs would it need, and how would you verify the output was right?

That's the whole thing. No prompt library, no platform debate.

What you end up with is a roadmap that ranks itself. The high-effort, high-frequency, KTBA tasks that score "yes" in Column D are your week-one wins — automate those and you buy back the time to actually do PTNF work.

AI is predictably good when the work is structured. This exercise is just you structuring the work before you point a model at it.

Four hours. That's the entire price of admission.
posts.voiceNotes: His operator-SOP register with numbered steps, but distinctly Yaseen — folds KTBA/PTNF and 'agreed-upon definition / verify the output' into the columns, and closes on the verbatim 'AI is predictably good when the work is structured.' Differentiated from Anthony's version of the same kernel.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: POCs Are the New Default — and Most Don't Convert
posts.postText:
The POC has quietly become the default first step in every enterprise AI deal.

And most of them die at the finish line.

Not because the product underdelivered. The technical proof usually lands. They die three weeks later in risk, compliance, and governance review — the conversation nobody scoped at the start.

The pattern I keep seeing across GTM leader conversations:

→ The POC proves the model works. Everyone's excited. Champion is bought in.
→ Then security sends the questionnaire, legal flags the data-handling terms, and IT asks where the model trains. None of it was qualified upfront.
→ The deal stalls in a queue it was never built to clear, and the forecast you marked commit slips two quarters.

Here's the reframe: a POC isn't a qualification stage. It's a technical demo wearing a qualification costume. If risk, compliance, and governance aren't in the room from the first call, you don't have a deal — you have a science project with a logo on it.

The fix is structural, not heroic:

✓ Stand up a trust portal and absorb the security questionnaire before the POC, not after.
✓ Map compliance and IT as co-buyers from qualification one.
✓ Score POCs on conversion-readiness, not just technical fit.

A POC that can't survive governance was never pipeline.

It was a really convincing way to feel busy.
posts.voiceNotes: Pattern-recognition opener from leader conversations, arrow bullets for the pattern + checkmark bullets for the fix (his functional-emoji habit), reframe-not-dunk energy, and a sharp declarative landing. Calm authority, no hype words.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Start Analog, Then Layer AI
posts.postText:
The best operators I know point AI at a problem only after they've written down what they think is true.

You can always tell when they didn't. The deck looks polished, then it collapses under the first probing question — because there was never a real point of view underneath it, just AI slop arranged neatly.

The build order is the opposite of how most people use these tools.

Analog first — your thinking, your structure. Then AI to find examples, crunch the data, and pressure-test the argument you already own.

Saved this one as a single graphic: the four-step sequence that keeps the work durable instead of brittle. Steal it for your next strategy doc.
posts.voiceNotes: His 'start in analog mode / AI slop' framing built around the build-order concept he loves; calm operator-educator tone, a single dense graphic, and a 'steal it' utility close rather than engagement bait.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A vertical 4-step flow titled 'Start Analog, Then Layer AI.' Four numbered rows (01–04), each a short label plus a one-line description, reading top to bottom as the sequence a sharp operator follows. Designed to be screenshotted and reused.

params.title: Start analog. Then layer AI.
params.body: 01 Write the hypothesis by hand · 02 AI finds examples + data · 03 AI pressure-tests your logic · 04 You decide. Skip step 01 and it collapses under the first hard question.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The Eisenhower Matrix for AI-Enabled Operators
posts.postText:
AI didn't just make my team faster this year. It made us capable of shipping ten times more work than the field can actually absorb.

That's the trap nobody warns you about. When output gets cheap, the temptation is to do everything. But three things that get adopted will always beat ten things that get ignored.

So we went back to a matrix Eisenhower would recognize — effort, urgency, importance — and added one axis for what AI can actually accelerate vs. what still needs human judgment.

Swipe through for the filter we're using to decide what to build, what to delegate to an agent, and what to leave alone entirely.

More on this in The LeanScale Letter.
posts.voiceNotes: Behind-the-scenes 'my team this year' anchor, the 'three things adopted beat ten ignored' thesis, PTNF-before-KTBA ordering, and the Letter CTA. Restrained caption that hands the payoff to the slides.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel on prioritizing in the AI era: the effort × impact filter with an AI multiplier on the effort axis, and how to pick the three things that actually move the needle.

params.title: The AI-Enabled Operator's Matrix.
params.body: How to prioritize when AI gives you 10x output and the field can only absorb a fraction of it.

params.title: Output is cheap. Absorption isn't.
params.body: AI multiplied what we can ship. It did nothing for how much change the field can actually adopt. That gap is where good operators go to drown.

params.title: Effort × importance, the original.
items: Drop — neither, and be honest about it

params.title: Add what AI can actually accelerate.
params.body: Lime = an agent does this well today against a clear definition. Purple = still needs your judgment. Score every task on both axes before you build.

params.title: Three things adopted beat ten things ignored.
params.body: Push the needle forward, then keep the boat afloat — in that order. More in The LeanScale Letter.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Start Analog, Then Layer AI
posts.postText:
Every strong AI workflow I've shipped started in a doc with no AI in it.

Sounds backwards. It isn't.

Here's the build order we use inside LeanScale Labs before any agent touches the work:

1. Write the thesis in analog mode. A doc, a whiteboard, a Google Sheet. Your actual argument, in your own words, before any model sees it.

2. Hand the model the thesis and let it find counter-examples. Not "write this for me" — "here's my position, now attack it and pull the data that proves or breaks it."

3. Let it crunch. Comparable accounts, benchmark numbers, the edge cases you forgot. This is where the leverage actually lives.

4. Pressure-test the output against the first probing question a CRO would ask. If the AI built the foundation, that question collapses the whole thing.

5. Rewrite the final in your voice. The model drafts, you own the judgment.

You can always tell when someone skipped step 1. The work reads fine until the first real question, then it falls apart because nobody actually thought it through. They just slopped it together.

The analog step is not the slow part. It's the part that makes everything after it hold up.

DM me if you want the doc template we start from.
posts.voiceNotes: Tactical playbook in Jake's builder lane — opens with a result/artifact statement (his hand-written hypothesis), numbered SOP with load-bearing specifics, lifts 'slopped it together' from the source quote, soft DM-the-template close. No contrarian hook, no emoji.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: POCs Are the New Default — and Most Don't Convert
posts.postText:
We watched a client's AI POC clear every technical bar, then die in security review three weeks later.

The product worked. The champion loved it. Then a 140-question security questionnaire landed at the finish line and the deal stalled for a quarter.

So we built the thing that should have existed before the POC ever started.

What shipped:

- A trust portal that hosts SOC 2, data handling, and model architecture docs in one link
- A library of pre-answered security and governance questions, mapped to the questionnaires we'd already seen kill deals
- An agent that takes an inbound security questionnaire, matches each item against the library, and drafts answers for human review in minutes instead of days
- A field on the opportunity that flags risk, compliance, and governance stakeholders at the qualification stage, not at contract review

The result on the next three POCs: security and procurement got looped in on call one. Questionnaire turnaround went from two weeks to an afternoon. Two of the three converted to enterprise.

The lesson I keep coming back to: POC-to-production isn't a product problem anymore. It's a governance-readiness problem. If you're absorbing those questions at the end, you've already lost the quarter.

If your POCs keep clearing the demo and dying in security review, the fix lives upstream of the POC.
posts.voiceNotes: Case study with concrete before/after numbers and a named artifact (trust portal + questionnaire agent) — Jake demonstrates competence, doesn't pitch. Opens with the result, bullets are short and concrete, builder register, soft DM close. Lifts the 'risk, compliance, governance' framing from the source kernel.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: AI-Native Services: The Quality Story Nobody Is Telling
posts.postText:
Most takes on AI in services obsess over headcount math. That's the boring story.

The interesting one is quality.

Here's a thing 10 years of building taught me: the bottleneck in services was never hours. It's holding the founding team's standard as you scale.

It always starts the same way. One or two people who are genuinely great, who care, who catch the thing nobody asked them to catch. The work is excellent because it runs through their hands.

Then you grow. You hire. The standard doesn't transfer cleanly, because it lived in someone's head, not in a system. Quality erodes one engagement at a time, and usually nobody notices until a customer does.

This is the part AI actually changes, and it has nothing to do with headcount.

When you encode the standard into a workflow — the diagnostic checklist, the data-model review, the QA pass an agent runs before anything ships — the founder's judgment stops being a single point of failure. It becomes infrastructure. The tenth engagement gets the same rigor as the first because the standard is in the system, not in a person who's now too busy to review everything.

AI doesn't let a services firm get bigger. Plenty of firms got bigger and worse.

It lets a small senior team stay excellent at scale. That's the unlock.
posts.voiceNotes: Soft educational reframe (not contrarian — respects Jake's avoidance of contrarian hooks). Earnest builder-teacher voice, lifts the 'erodes as you grow' source quote and the '10 years of engineering taught me' sample phrasing, plain declarative sentences, mental-model landing. No emoji, no CTA pitch.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Forward Deployed Engineers Are Not a Trend — They're a Requirement
posts.postText:
Spent last week comparing implementation models from a handful of AI vendors a customer was evaluating.

One number told the whole story: the FDE-to-account ratio.

The vendors shipping forward deployed engineers — people who write code, own the data model, and sit in the customer's Slack — were converting POCs to production at roughly double the rate of the ones who handed implementation to an SI partner.

This is the part of the AI services story that never makes it onto LinkedIn. It's not a hire. It's a stage of the customer journey, and it belongs in your growth model as a CAC line, not a post-sale afterthought.

Made one graphic on the ratio that matters. Save it for your next vendor eval.

What ratio are you running? Curious where teams have landed.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry-observation register delivered as a single dense stat graphic — Jake's artifact-heavy 'show the work' instinct. Opens with the activity, not a take; lifts 'sit in the customer's Slack' and 'CAC line, not a post-sale afterthought' from the FDE kernel. Practitioner-facing question close, no emoji.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A single big-stat graphic. One huge centered figure — '~2×' — as the POC-to-production conversion delta between in-house forward deployed engineers and outsourced implementation. Label beneath: 'POC-to-production conversion: in-house FDEs vs. outsourced implementation.' Plus a small supporting ratio line: '1 FDE : 5–7 accounts.'

params.title: The FDE ratio that decides POC outcomes.
params.statLabel: POC-to-production conversion: in-house FDEs vs. outsourced
params.body: Vendors with forward deployed engineers convert at roughly double the rate. Sustainable gearing: 1 FDE per 5-7 accounts. Model it as a CAC line, not a post-sale afterthought.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Why, What, How: The Three Layers of GTM Work
posts.postText:
Founders keep telling me their AI engineer makes a RevOps leader redundant.

As the person who'd be that engineer: they're wrong, and it's about to cost them an expensive year.

GTM work has three layers, and a GTM engineer only owns one of them.

The Why belongs to the CEO and CRO. The What belongs to RevOps. The How belongs to engineering — that's my layer, and I can automate it all day.

What I can't do is decide what the What should be. That takes someone who's watched a comp plan break and sat through a forecast call. Skip that layer and you ship a beautifully engineered system that solves the wrong problem.

Built this out as a 5-slide breakdown — the whole framework's in the swipe.
posts.voiceNotes: Carousel that decomposes cleanly into a Why/What/How numbered sequence — Jake's strong-fit format. Caption opens with the overheard objection (show, don't take), pragmatic builder register, names his own layer honestly ('that's my layer'), soft DM-the-diagram close. No emoji. Slides reinforce the arc without restating the caption verbatim.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: A 5-slide carousel on the three layers of GTM work from an engineer's seat — why automating the How (engineering's layer) never replaces the What (RevOps owns it).

params.title: Why / What / How.
params.body: The three layers of GTM work — and why one AI engineer can't cover all of them.

params.title: The Why — owned by CEO & CRO.
params.body: What market we attack, what the motion is, what winning means this year. Strategy. Nobody automates this.

params.title: The What — owned by RevOps.
params.body: Lead lifecycle, territory model, comp plan, forecast cadence. The translation of strategy into process. The layer teams keep cutting.

params.title: The How — owned by GTM Engineering.
params.body: Systems, integrations, agents, automations that make the What run at scale. This is my layer. I can build all of it.

params.title: Skip the What and the How solves the wrong problem.
params.body: A perfectly engineered system, pointed at the wrong target. DM me for the diagram.
